eli3: The jackknife (Quenouille 1949, Tukey 1958) estimates the variance and bias of a statistic by systematically leaving out one observation at a time. Compute the statistic on each of the n leave-one-out samples of size nвҲ’1, then combine the resulting n jackknife estimates to estimate SE and bias вҖ” without any resampling randomness.

The jackknife bias estimate: Bias_jack = (nвҲ’1)(ОёМ„вӮӢ вҲ’ ОёМӮ), where ОёМ„вӮӢ = (1/n)ОЈОёМӮвӮӢбөў is the mean of the leave-one-out estimates. The bias-corrected jackknife estimate: ОёМӮ_jack = nОёМӮ вҲ’ (nвҲ’1)ОёМ„вӮӢ = ОёМӮ вҲ’ Bias_jack. This reduces the O(1/n) bias term in many estimators to O(1/nВІ).

The jackknife is deterministic (no random resampling), computationally cheaper than bootstrap (only n leave-one-out evaluations vs B), and historically important. Its main limitation: it fails for non-smooth statistics (median, quantiles, max) because removing one observation may not change the statistic at all вҖ” the jackknife variance estimate is zero or misleading.

whenWhy.title: Bias correction for smooth estimators
whenWhy.body: For estimators with O(1/n) bias (MLE of variance, correlation coefficient, many nonlinear estimators), the jackknife bias correction eliminates the leading bias term. Quenouille's original application was bias reduction for ratio estimators in survey sampling. The jackknife is exact for linear statistics and consistent for smooth nonlinear statistics.

whenWhy.title: Variance estimation for survey statistics
whenWhy.body: The jackknife is the standard variance estimation method for complex survey designs (delete-a-group jackknife, JK1, JKn). For stratified multi-stage designs: delete one PSU from each stratum in turn and compute the survey statistic on the reduced sample. The jackknife properly accounts for stratification and clustering in variance estimation вҖ” used in NHANES, NAEP, and most national surveys.

whenWhy.title: Acceleration estimation for BCa bootstrap CI
whenWhy.body: The acceleration constant Гў in the BCa bootstrap CI is estimated via the jackknife: Гў = (1/6)ОЈбөў(ОёМ„вӮӢ вҲ’ ОёМӮвӮӢбөў)Ві / [ОЈбөў(ОёМ„вӮӢ вҲ’ ОёМӮвӮӢбөў)ВІ]^{3/2}. The jackknife provides a computationally efficient way to estimate the rate at which the SE of ОёМӮ changes with the true parameter value вҖ” a key component of the BCa correction.

deepDiveIntro: Define ОёМӮвӮӢбөў = T(xвӮҒ,вҖҰ,xбөўвӮӢвӮҒ,xбөўвӮҠвӮҒ,вҖҰ,xвӮҷ) as the estimator with observation i removed, for i = 1,вҖҰ,n. The jackknife estimate of variance: Var_jack(ОёМӮ) = [(nвҲ’1)/n]ОЈбөў(ОёМӮвӮӢбөў вҲ’ ОёМ„вӮӢ)ВІ, where ОёМ„вӮӢ = (1/n)ОЈОёМӮвӮӢбөў. The n pseudovalues are ОёМғбөў = nОёМӮ вҲ’ (nвҲ’1)ОёМӮвӮӢбөў; then Var_jack(ОёМӮ) = (1/(n(nвҲ’1)))ОЈбөў(ОёМғбөў вҲ’ ОёМӮ_jack)ВІ.

For a smooth functional T with influence function в„“(xбөў;F): ОёМӮвӮӢбөў вүҲ ОёМӮ вҲ’ в„“(xбөў)/n. So ОёМӮвӮӢбөў вҲ’ ОёМ„вӮӢ вүҲ вҲ’(в„“(xбөў) вҲ’ в„“М„)/n and Var_jack(ОёМӮ) вүҲ (1/nВІ)ОЈбөў(в„“(xбөў) вҲ’ в„“М„)ВІ вҖ” the variance of the influence function divided by n. This is the sandwich estimator for nonparametric variance.

deepDive.heading: Jackknife Variance Estimator
deepDive.body: Var_jack(ОёМӮ) = [(nвҲ’1)/n]ОЈбөўвӮҢвӮҒвҒҝ (ОёМӮвӮӢбөў вҲ’ ОёМ„вӮӢ)ВІ. The factor (nвҲ’1)/n corrects for the reduced sample size in each leave-one-out estimate. For the sample mean xМ„: ОёМӮвӮӢбөў = (nxМ„ вҲ’ xбөў)/(nвҲ’1); ОёМ„вӮӢ = xМ„; Var_jack = [(nвҲ’1)/n]В·ОЈ(xМ„ вҲ’ (nxМ„вҲ’xбөў)/(nвҲ’1))ВІ/n = sВІ/n вҖ” exactly the standard variance formula. Jackknife is exact for linear statistics. [1]

deepDive.heading: Bias Reduction via Jackknife
deepDive.body: If E[ОёМӮ] = Оё + aвӮҒ/n + aвӮӮ/nВІ + вҖҰ, then E[ОёМӮвӮӢбөў] = Оё + aвӮҒ/(nвҲ’1) + вҖҰ. The jackknife estimate ОёМӮ_jack = nОёМӮ вҲ’ (nвҲ’1)ОёМ„вӮӢ has E[ОёМӮ_jack] = Оё + O(1/nВІ) вҖ” the O(1/n) bias is removed. Example: sample variance ПғМӮВІ = ОЈ(xбөўвҲ’xМ„)ВІ/n has bias вҲ’ПғВІ/n; the jackknife-corrected estimate = ОЈ(xбөўвҲ’xМ„)ВІ/(nвҲ’1) = sВІ (the unbiased version). [1]

deepDive.heading: Jackknife vs Bootstrap
deepDive.body: Jackknife: deterministic (no randomness), n evaluations, first-order accurate, fails for non-smooth statistics. Bootstrap: randomized (B evaluations), flexible, BCa is second-order accurate, can handle any statistic. For smooth statistics and large n: jackknife SE вүҲ bootstrap SE. For the median (non-smooth): jackknife gives Var_jack = 0 (removing any one observation doesn't change the median for a large n), while bootstrap correctly estimates the variance. Default recommendation: use bootstrap; use jackknife for survey variance estimation and the BCa acceleration constant. [1]

deepDive.heading: Delete-d Jackknife
deepDive.body: Delete-d jackknife (Wu, 1990): remove d > 1 observations at a time. With d = 1: standard jackknife. With d вҶ’ n: approaches bootstrap behavior. For d growing with n (e.g., d = n^{2/3}), the delete-d jackknife is consistent for non-smooth statistics like quantiles where delete-1 jackknife fails. The m-out-of-n bootstrap achieves similar goals via resampling m << n observations. [1]

takeaways: Jackknife: leave out one observation at a time; Var_jack = [(nвҲ’1)/n]ОЈ(ОёМӮвӮӢбөў вҲ’ ОёМ„вӮӢ)ВІ. [1]
Bias correction: ОёМӮ_jack = nОёМӮ вҲ’ (nвҲ’1)ОёМ„вӮӢ; removes O(1/n) bias term. [1]
Deterministic and requires only n evaluations; cheaper than bootstrap. [1]
Fails for non-smooth statistics (median, quantiles, max) вҖ” use bootstrap instead. [1]
Key use case: survey variance estimation (delete-a-group jackknife) and BCa acceleration constant. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does the jackknife fail for the sample median?
edgeCases.a: The sample median ОёМӮ = x_(вҢҲn/2вҢү) is not a smooth functional of the empirical distribution. Removing one observation typically doesn't change the median value at all (for odd n, the median is unchanged unless the removed observation was the median itself). So ОёМӮвӮӢбөў вүҲ ОёМӮ for most i, giving Var_jack вүҲ 0. This is incorrect вҖ” the true sampling variance of the median is ПҖ/(2n) В· (1/f(Оё))ВІ where f is the density at the median. The bootstrap correctly estimates this via the distribution of bootstrap medians. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the relationship between the jackknife and the influence function?
edgeCases.a: The influence function IF(x;T,F) = lim_{ОөвҶ’0} (T(F_Оө) вҲ’ T(F))/Оө measures the effect of an infinitesimal contamination at point x on the estimator T, where F_Оө = (1вҲ’Оө)F + ОөОҙ_x. The empirical influence value for observation i is approximately: (nвҲ’1)(ОёМӮвӮӢбөў вҲ’ ОёМӮ) вүҲ вҲ’IF(xбөў;T,FМӮ). So the jackknife provides finite-sample approximations to influence values, connecting it to robust statistics and the sandwich variance estimator. [1]

sources.label: Efron & Hastie вҖ” Computer Age Statistical Inference, Ch. 11
Davison & Hinkley вҖ” Bootstrap Methods and Their Application, Ch. 2
